title: BUS 340 — Information Systems Management
description: The full BUS 340 curriculum at NC State. Understand how organizations design, use, and manage information systems — from databases and enterprise software through cybersecurity, AI, and digital transformation.
subject: Information Systems

learn.explanation: An information system (IS) is a set of interrelated components that collect, process, store, and distribute information to support decision-making and control in an organization. The five core components are people, processes, data, hardware, and software/networks. Importantly, IS is broader than IT — IT refers to the technical infrastructure alone, while IS includes the human and organizational layer on top of it.
learn.example: Amazon's order fulfillment system is a classic IS. The hardware and software are the IT components. But the IS includes warehouse workers following picking processes, managers using dashboards to spot bottlenecks, and business rules that trigger automatic reorders when stock drops below a threshold.
keyPoints: An IS = people + processes + data + hardware + software working together toward an organizational goal
IT is a subset of IS — the technical components only. IS adds people and process to make technology useful
IS fails most often at the people and process layer, not the technical layer — adoption and alignment matter more than hardware

learn.explanation: Organizations use different IS types for different levels of decision-making. Transaction Processing Systems (TPS) handle daily operations. Management Information Systems (MIS) aggregate TPS data into reports for middle managers. Decision Support Systems (DSS) help managers analyze options. Executive Information Systems (EIS) give senior leaders high-level strategic dashboards.
learn.example: A hotel chain uses a TPS to record every room booking. The MIS aggregates this into weekly occupancy reports for regional managers. A DSS lets the revenue manager model pricing strategies. The EIS gives the CEO a dashboard showing portfolio-wide occupancy and revenue trends.
keyPoints: TPS handles operational transactions — the foundation all other IS types depend on
MIS aggregates TPS data into scheduled reports for middle management
DSS and EIS serve higher-level decisions — less structured, more analytical, more strategic

learn.explanation: IS creates business value through operational efficiency, decision quality, and competitive advantage. The third is most strategically important. When IS capabilities are rare and hard to imitate — like Netflix's recommendation engine or Amazon's fulfillment network — they become sustained competitive advantages.
learn.example: Zara's IS connects point-of-sale data from 2,000+ stores directly to design and production teams, enabling a two-week design-to-shelf cycle vs. the industry standard of six months. Competitors cannot replicate it quickly because it requires simultaneous changes to systems, supplier relationships, and organizational processes.
keyPoints: IS value = efficiency + decision quality + competitive advantage — the third is hardest to build and most durable
Competitive IS advantage requires rare, hard-to-imitate capabilities — not just better software
IS that every competitor uses equally creates no differentiation

learn.explanation: A business process is a structured set of activities that transforms inputs into outputs to achieve a business goal. Every process has inputs (materials, data, requests), activities (steps performed by people or systems), outputs (products, decisions, records), and feedback loops (information that improves future process runs). Understanding processes is prerequisite to improving them with IS.
learn.example: An order fulfillment process at Nike takes inputs (customer order, inventory levels), runs through activities (order verification, warehouse picking, shipping label generation, carrier handoff), produces outputs (delivered product, updated inventory records), and uses feedback (delivery confirmation, return rates) to improve future fulfillment.
keyPoints: Every process has inputs, activities, outputs, and feedback — IS can improve any of these four elements
Cross-functional processes that span departments are where IS integration creates the most value
You cannot improve a process you haven't mapped — process documentation comes before IS design

learn.explanation: IS improves business processes by automating repetitive steps, eliminating handoff delays, surfacing bottlenecks through data, and enabling real-time coordination across departments. The key principle is that IS should be designed around the process, not the other way around — organizations that redesign their processes to fit software often end up with worse outcomes than before.
learn.example: A hospital's patient discharge process previously took 4 hours due to manual coordination between nurses, billing, and pharmacy. An IS that automatically notifies all three departments simultaneously when a physician enters a discharge order reduces this to 90 minutes — not by speeding up each step, but by eliminating the sequential waiting between them.
keyPoints: IS eliminates waiting time between process steps — this is often larger than the time spent on the steps themselves
Automating a broken process makes it faster and more broken — fix the process design before automating
The biggest IS process gains come from cross-functional integration, not within-department automation

learn.explanation: Data is raw, unprocessed facts. Information is data that has been processed and given context so it is meaningful. Knowledge is information combined with experience and judgment to enable action. The transformation chain is: data → information → knowledge → insight. IS systems are fundamentally in the business of moving organizations up this chain.
learn.example: '47000' is data. 'Q3 sales were $47,000' is information. 'Q3 sales have declined 12% for three consecutive years, suggesting a structural problem rather than a seasonal dip' is knowledge. 'We should restructure our sales territory before Q4' is insight. Each level requires more processing and human judgment.
keyPoints: Data alone has no business value — value comes from transforming data into information and knowledge
More data does not automatically produce better decisions — the transformation process determines quality
IS design should work backward from the decisions that need to be made, not forward from the data available

learn.explanation: A relational database organizes data into tables with rows (records) and columns (fields). Tables are linked through keys — a primary key uniquely identifies each record in a table, and a foreign key in one table references the primary key of another, creating relationships between tables. This structure eliminates data redundancy and enables powerful querying across related data.
learn.example: A customer orders database has a Customers table (CustomerID as primary key, name, email) and an Orders table (OrderID as primary key, CustomerID as foreign key, product, amount). The foreign key links every order to its customer without duplicating customer data in every order record.
keyPoints: Primary keys uniquely identify records; foreign keys create relationships between tables
Relational structure eliminates redundancy — customer data lives in one place, referenced everywhere it's needed
SQL is the standard language for querying relational databases — even conceptual fluency is valuable for business analysts

learn.explanation: Data quality refers to how well data is fit for its intended use. Key dimensions are accuracy (is it correct?), completeness (is anything missing?), consistency (does it match across systems?), and timeliness (is it current?). Data governance is the organizational framework of policies, roles, and processes that maintain data quality over time.
learn.example: A retailer with customer addresses stored in three systems — CRM, shipping, and loyalty program — finds that 23% of records conflict across systems. A data governance initiative assigns data stewards responsible for each domain, establishes a master customer record as the authoritative source, and creates processes to sync updates across systems within 24 hours.
keyPoints: Bad data quality is invisible until it causes a visible failure — proactive governance prevents costly downstream errors
Data governance assigns ownership — someone must be accountable for each data domain's quality
The most common data quality problems are duplicates, missing values, and inconsistent formatting across systems

learn.explanation: A data warehouse is a centralized repository that consolidates data from multiple operational systems for analysis and reporting. Unlike operational databases optimized for transactions, data warehouses are optimized for queries across large historical datasets. A data mart is a subset of a data warehouse focused on a specific business function like marketing or finance.
learn.example: Walmart's operational systems (POS, inventory, HR, supply chain) each generate transaction data in real time. A data warehouse pulls from all these systems nightly, standardizes the data, and stores years of history. The marketing team accesses a data mart — a filtered subset — to analyze customer purchase patterns without querying the full warehouse.
keyPoints: Data warehouses consolidate historical data from multiple systems — operational databases store current transactions only
Data marts give specific teams fast access to relevant data without exposing the full warehouse
ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) is the process of moving data from operational systems into the warehouse

learn.explanation: Customer Relationship Management (CRM) systems manage all interactions between a company and its customers across the full lifecycle — from lead generation through sales, service, and retention. CRM centralizes customer data so every employee interacting with a customer has full context. The business value is revenue growth through better sales execution and customer retention.
learn.example: Salesforce CRM at a software company tracks every email, call, demo, and contract with each prospect. When a sales rep leaves, their replacement opens the CRM and has complete history. The system also flags customers who haven't logged in for 30 days as churn risks, triggering automated outreach from customer success.
keyPoints: CRM is customer-facing — it manages relationships and revenue. Don't confuse it with ERP which manages internal operations
CRM value depends entirely on data quality — a CRM reps don't update is worse than no CRM
The three CRM functions are operational (automating sales/service), analytical (understanding customer behavior), and collaborative (sharing customer data across departments)

learn.explanation: Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) systems integrate core business functions — finance, HR, manufacturing, supply chain, procurement — into a single system with a shared database. The integration eliminates data silos and enables real-time visibility across the entire organization. ERP implementations are among the largest and riskiest IS projects a company undertakes.
learn.example: Before SAP ERP, a manufacturer's finance team waited two weeks for month-end inventory reports compiled manually from warehouse spreadsheets. After ERP, finance sees inventory values update in real time as warehouse staff scan items. The same transaction that updates inventory automatically updates the general ledger — no manual reconciliation needed.
keyPoints: ERP integrates internal operations across all departments through a shared database — one version of the truth
ERP implementations fail most often due to inadequate change management, not technical problems
The main ERP tradeoff is integration vs. flexibility — ERP enforces standardized processes that may not fit every department's needs

learn.explanation: Supply Chain Management (SCM) systems coordinate the flow of goods, information, and money from raw material suppliers through production to end customers. SCM systems provide visibility across the supply chain, enable demand forecasting, optimize inventory levels, and manage supplier relationships. The bullwhip effect — where small demand fluctuations amplify into large supply swings — is a classic problem SCM systems help prevent.
learn.example: Toyota's SCM system shares real-time production schedules with suppliers, so component deliveries arrive hours before they're needed on the assembly line rather than sitting in a warehouse. When a natural disaster disrupted a supplier in 2011, the SCM system immediately identified which vehicles were affected and rerouted sourcing — a process that would have taken weeks manually.
keyPoints: SCM visibility across the full chain prevents the bullwhip effect by sharing real demand data with all suppliers
The tension in SCM is efficiency vs. resilience — lean inventory reduces cost but increases disruption risk
SCM systems integrate with ERP internally and EDI (Electronic Data Interchange) externally with suppliers

learn.explanation: Human Resource Information Systems (HRIS) manage employee data, payroll, benefits, recruitment, and performance management. Financial IS manage accounting, budgeting, accounts payable/receivable, and financial reporting. Both are often modules within a larger ERP system. These systems are heavily regulated — compliance with tax law, labor law, and financial reporting standards (GAAP) is a primary design constraint.
learn.example: Workday HRIS at a 5,000-person company manages the full employee lifecycle: job postings, applicant tracking, onboarding paperwork, time tracking, payroll calculation, benefits enrollment, and performance reviews. When an employee is promoted, the HRIS automatically updates their pay grade, tax withholding, and org chart position simultaneously.
keyPoints: HRIS and financial systems are compliance-driven — regulatory requirements shape their design as much as business needs
Payroll is the most critical HRIS function — errors have immediate legal and trust consequences
Financial IS produce the outputs required for external reporting — errors here affect audits, investor relations, and legal compliance

units.title: Cloud Computing and Modern Business Tools

learn.explanation: Cloud computing delivers computing resources — servers, storage, databases, software — over the internet on a pay-per-use basis. The three service models are IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service: raw computing resources), PaaS (Platform as a Service: development environment), and SaaS (Software as a Service: ready-to-use applications). Deployment models are public (shared infrastructure), private (dedicated infrastructure), and hybrid (combination).
learn.example: Netflix runs entirely on AWS (Amazon's public cloud IaaS). Instead of owning data centers, Netflix rents servers by the hour — scaling up to 10x capacity during peak evening hours and scaling back overnight. This would be impossible with owned infrastructure. Their video encoding tools run on PaaS, and their HR team uses Workday SaaS.
keyPoints: IaaS = rent servers; PaaS = rent a development platform; SaaS = rent finished software — each abstracts away more complexity
Public cloud is shared and cheaper; private cloud is dedicated and more controlled; hybrid combines both for different workloads
The shift from capital expenditure (buying servers) to operating expenditure (renting capacity) is the core financial model change cloud enables

learn.explanation: Cloud benefits include scalability (expand or contract instantly), cost flexibility (pay only for what you use), accessibility (work from anywhere), and faster deployment (no hardware procurement). Cloud risks include vendor lock-in (hard to switch providers after deep integration), downtime dependency (if the provider goes down, you go down), data security concerns (sensitive data on third-party infrastructure), and compliance complexity (data residency laws vary by country).
learn.example: Dropbox initially built on AWS for fast scaling. After growing to millions of users, they calculated that building their own infrastructure would save $75M over two years — but the migration cost and vendor lock-in risk were substantial. They ultimately built a hybrid: proprietary infrastructure for most storage, AWS for overflow and flexibility.
keyPoints: Scalability is cloud's primary operational benefit — matching capacity to demand in real time is impossible with owned hardware
Vendor lock-in is the most underestimated cloud risk — proprietary services (AWS Lambda, Google BigQuery) are hard to migrate away from
Data sovereignty is a growing compliance risk — many countries require certain data to remain within their borders

learn.explanation: An API (Application Programming Interface) is a defined way for two software systems to communicate. APIs allow businesses to connect systems, share data, and build on each other's capabilities without sharing underlying code. Modern business IS ecosystems are built on API integrations — CRM talking to email, ERP talking to shipping carriers, payment processors talking to e-commerce platforms.
learn.example: When you book an Uber, APIs coordinate in milliseconds: Google Maps API provides routing, Stripe API processes payment, Twilio API sends your SMS confirmation, and Uber's internal APIs connect driver location to passenger app. None of these companies built mapping, payments, or messaging from scratch — they integrated best-in-class services via APIs.
keyPoints: APIs enable composable business systems — assemble best-in-class tools rather than building everything in-house
API dependency is a business risk — if a critical third-party API changes or goes down, your system breaks
REST APIs are the dominant modern standard — understanding what they do conceptually is more important than knowing how to build them

learn.explanation: Low-code platforms require minimal programming; no-code platforms require none — both enable non-developers to build applications through visual interfaces. These tools democratize software creation, allowing business analysts and operations staff to automate workflows without IT involvement. The tradeoff is speed and accessibility vs. flexibility and scalability.
learn.example: A marketing operations manager at HubSpot uses Zapier (no-code) to automatically add every new webinar registrant to a Salesforce CRM campaign, send a confirmation email, and post a Slack notification to the sales team — a workflow that would have required a developer sprint six years ago. The limitation: when they needed custom logic for enterprise accounts, Zapier couldn't handle it and they needed a developer anyway.
keyPoints: No-code/low-code is ideal for automating repetitive workflows between existing systems — not for building complex custom applications
Shadow IT risk: business units building their own tools create data security and governance problems IT doesn't know about
The citizen developer trend is real but has limits — complex logic, security requirements, and scale still require professional development

learn.explanation: Business Intelligence (BI) refers to the systems, processes, and tools that transform raw data into actionable insights for business decisions. BI systems include dashboards (real-time visual displays of KPIs), reports (scheduled summaries of performance), and ad-hoc query tools (flexible exploration of data). KPIs (Key Performance Indicators) are the specific metrics a dashboard tracks — choosing the right KPIs is as important as building the dashboard itself.
learn.example: A Walmart store manager's BI dashboard shows real-time sales vs. target, inventory turnover by category, and staff hours vs. budget. The dashboard doesn't show everything — it shows the 8 metrics the manager can actually act on during a shift. A poorly designed BI dashboard shows 40 metrics and obscures the 3 that matter.
keyPoints: BI value depends on KPI selection — measuring the wrong things perfectly is worse than measuring the right things imprecisely
Dashboards are for monitoring; reports are for review; ad-hoc tools are for investigation — each serves a different decision type
The biggest BI failure mode is building dashboards nobody uses because they don't match actual decision workflows

learn.explanation: There are four types of analytics forming a progression from descriptive to prescriptive. Descriptive analytics answers 'what happened?' Diagnostic analytics answers 'why did it happen?' Predictive analytics answers 'what will happen?' Prescriptive analytics answers 'what should we do?' Each type requires more sophisticated IS and produces more actionable output — but also requires more data and analytical capability.
learn.example: A retailer analyzes a sales drop. Descriptive: sales fell 18% in Q3. Diagnostic: the drop concentrated in the Northeast region after a competitor opened nearby. Predictive: if the competitor expands to two more regions, sales will fall 12% there too. Prescriptive: open a loyalty program in at-risk regions before the competitor arrives, projected to retain 70% of at-risk revenue.
keyPoints: Most organizations are strong at descriptive and weak at predictive/prescriptive — the higher levels require better data infrastructure and analytical talent
Diagnostic analytics is where most business value is lost — organizations see what happened but don't invest in understanding why
Prescriptive analytics requires not just data but a model of how interventions affect outcomes — this is where IS and business strategy intersect

learn.explanation: Data visualization translates numbers into visual form to make patterns and relationships immediately apparent. Core principles: choose chart type to match the data relationship (bar for comparison, line for trend, scatter for correlation), maximize the data-ink ratio (remove visual elements that don't represent data), start bar charts at zero to preserve proportional accuracy, and use color purposefully rather than decoratively.
learn.example: A CFO presents revenue growth using a bar chart with a y-axis starting at $47M instead of $0. A 3% increase looks like a 200% increase visually. The same data on a zero-based axis shows the increase as the minor fluctuation it actually is. The first chart is technically accurate but visually misleading — a common and consequential visualization error.
keyPoints: Chart type selection is a communication decision — the wrong chart type obscures the insight even with correct data
Truncated y-axes are the most common misleading visualization technique — always start bar charts at zero
High data-ink ratio means every visual element earns its place — remove gridlines, borders, and decorations that don't add information

learn.explanation: The CIA triad is the foundation of information security: Confidentiality (only authorized users access data), Integrity (data is accurate and unaltered), and Availability (systems are accessible when needed). Authentication verifies identity (who are you?); authorization controls access (what can you do?). Multi-factor authentication (MFA) requires two or more verification factors — something you know, something you have, something you are.
learn.example: A hospital IS must protect all three CIA dimensions: patient records must be confidential (only treating physicians access them), integrity must be maintained (a tampered medication dosage could kill a patient), and availability is critical (a ransomware attack that locks IS during surgery is life-threatening). Most organizations focus on confidentiality and neglect availability until an outage occurs.
keyPoints: CIA triad: Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability — security failures damage one or more of these three properties
Authentication and authorization are distinct — verifying identity is separate from controlling what that identity can access
MFA dramatically reduces account compromise risk — the second factor stops most credential-based attacks even when passwords are stolen

learn.explanation: Social engineering attacks exploit human psychology rather than technical vulnerabilities. Phishing uses deceptive emails to steal credentials or install malware. Malware is malicious software including viruses, ransomware (encrypts data and demands payment), and spyware. Incident response is the structured process for detecting, containing, and recovering from security incidents — organizations without a response plan suffer significantly worse outcomes when breaches occur.
learn.example: The 2017 WannaCry ransomware attack encrypted IS at 200,000+ organizations across 150 countries. Hospitals diverted ambulances. FedEx lost $300M. The attack exploited a known Windows vulnerability that many organizations hadn't patched. The lesson: most successful attacks exploit known vulnerabilities and human error, not sophisticated zero-day exploits.
keyPoints: Human error is the attack vector in over 80% of breaches — technical controls alone are insufficient without security awareness training
Ransomware's business impact goes beyond ransom payment — downtime, recovery costs, and reputational damage typically exceed the ransom itself
Incident response planning before an attack determines recovery speed — organizations that practice response plans recover in days; those that don't recover in weeks

learn.explanation: Data privacy gives individuals control over their personal information — who collects it, how it's used, and how long it's retained. Key principles include informed consent (people know what data is collected and agree), data minimization (collect only what's necessary), and purpose limitation (use data only for its stated purpose). Algorithmic bias occurs when IS systems produce systematically unfair outcomes for certain groups due to biased training data or design choices.
learn.example: Amazon scrapped an AI hiring tool in 2018 after discovering it penalized resumes containing the word 'women's' and downgraded graduates of all-women's colleges. The system was trained on historical hiring data that reflected past male-dominated hiring patterns. Technically functional, ethically broken — and a liability risk. This is algorithmic bias with real organizational consequences.
keyPoints: Privacy is not just a legal compliance issue — organizations that treat customer data extractively erode trust and face growing regulatory exposure
Algorithmic bias is an IS design problem, not just a social problem — it requires intentional testing and diverse development teams to catch
Data minimization reduces both privacy risk and breach impact — data you don't collect can't be stolen or misused

learn.explanation: IS operates within a legal framework that varies by industry and geography. Key frameworks include GDPR (EU data protection regulation with global reach), HIPAA (US healthcare data privacy), SOX (financial reporting controls for public companies), and PCI-DSS (payment card security standards). Intellectual property law protects software through copyright and patents. Non-compliance carries financial penalties, reputational damage, and in some cases criminal liability.
learn.example: A US e-commerce company expanding to Europe discovers that its data practices violate GDPR — it stores EU customer data on US servers without adequate protection and uses it for marketing without explicit consent. Compliance requires redesigning data flows, adding consent mechanisms, and potentially moving data storage. The cost of compliance is significant; the cost of a GDPR fine (up to 4% of global revenue) is potentially larger.
keyPoints: GDPR has global reach — any organization handling EU residents' data must comply regardless of where the organization is based
Compliance is a floor not a ceiling — meeting legal minimums doesn't mean IS is ethical or trustworthy
IS architects must consider regulatory requirements at design time — retrofitting compliance into existing systems is far more expensive than building it in

learn.explanation: Artificial intelligence in business encompasses machine learning (systems that improve from data without explicit programming), generative AI (systems that create new content), and recommendation systems (systems that predict preferences). AI creates business value by automating pattern recognition at scale, personalizing experiences, and augmenting human decision-making. AI risks include hallucination (confident wrong outputs), bias, privacy violations, and over-reliance.
learn.example: Spotify's recommendation system analyzes 600 billion data points daily to generate personalized playlists. It doesn't understand music — it recognizes patterns in what listeners with similar tastes play together. This IS capability drives 30% of all listening on the platform and is a core competitive advantage that took years of data accumulation to build.
keyPoints: AI excels at pattern recognition in large datasets — tasks that are tedious for humans but don't require judgment or creativity
Hallucination is the critical AI risk for business use — AI systems generate plausible-sounding wrong answers with high confidence
AI augments human decisions most safely when humans review outputs — fully automated high-stakes decisions without human oversight are the highest-risk deployment

learn.explanation: Robotic Process Automation (RPA) uses software bots to automate repetitive, rule-based tasks that previously required human interaction with IS — copying data between systems, filling forms, processing invoices. RPA works on top of existing systems without requiring integration. Chatbots handle routine customer service interactions. Human-in-the-loop systems keep humans involved in decisions above a certain risk or complexity threshold.
learn.example: A bank's accounts payable team previously had staff manually copy invoice data from PDFs into the ERP system — 400 invoices per day, 3 minutes each, 20 hours of labor daily. RPA bots now handle 85% of invoices automatically, with humans reviewing only the 15% the bot flags as ambiguous. Labor redirected to exception handling and vendor relationship management.
keyPoints: RPA automates the interaction with existing systems — it doesn't replace them or require integration APIs
RPA is brittle — bots break when the underlying system's interface changes, requiring maintenance investment
Human-in-the-loop is not a failure of automation — it is the correct design for high-stakes decisions where errors have significant consequences

learn.explanation: Global IS must navigate technical, cultural, and regulatory complexity that domestic systems avoid. Localization requires adapting IS for local languages, currencies, date formats, and business practices. Data sovereignty laws require certain data to remain within national borders. Outsourcing and offshoring IS development or operations introduces coordination costs, quality risks, and intellectual property concerns.
learn.example: McDonald's global IS must handle 40,000+ locations across 100+ countries with different tax systems, currencies, labor laws, and menu items. Their POS system is localized for each market while maintaining global data standards that allow corporate to compare performance across markets. A price change in Japan cannot use the same tax calculation logic as France.
keyPoints: Global IS complexity is multiplicative not additive — each new country adds regulatory, cultural, and technical dimensions that interact with each other
Data sovereignty is becoming a primary IS architecture constraint — where data lives is increasingly a legal question not just a technical one
Outsourcing IS reduces cost but transfers control — organizations that outsource core IS capabilities often find themselves dependent on vendors for strategic functions

learn.explanation: Digital transformation is the fundamental redesign of business models, processes, and customer experiences using digital technology — not just digitizing existing processes but rethinking what the business does and how it creates value. It requires change management, leadership commitment, and tolerance for disruption. Most digital transformations fail not because of technology but because of organizational resistance and unclear strategy.
learn.example: Best Buy's digital transformation in the early 2010s is a survival story. Facing Amazon's competition, they didn't just build a better website — they redesigned their store model as showrooms with price-match guarantees, retrained staff as technology advisors, and built vendor-in-store partnerships. The IS changes (inventory integration, customer data platform, omnichannel fulfillment) were in service of a new business model, not ends in themselves.
keyPoints: Digital transformation is a business strategy with IS as the enabler — organizations that treat it as an IT project almost always fail
Legacy systems are the most common technical obstacle — replacing them is expensive and risky, but operating around them limits transformation
Change management is the most underinvested element of digital transformation — technology changes faster than people and culture do

learn.explanation: Porter's value chain identifies primary activities (inbound logistics, operations, outbound logistics, marketing/sales, service) and support activities (infrastructure, HR, technology, procurement) where IS can create value. Switching costs are the expenses customers incur when changing providers — IS that embeds deeply into customer workflows creates switching costs that protect revenue. Network effects occur when a product becomes more valuable as more people use it.
learn.example: Salesforce creates switching costs by becoming the system of record for customer relationships — years of data, custom workflows, and staff training make switching painful regardless of competitor pricing. LinkedIn creates network effects — its value grows with every new member because everyone's network expands. Both are IS-enabled competitive mechanisms that are hard to replicate.
keyPoints: Porter's value chain helps identify where IS investment creates most value for a specific business model
Switching costs and network effects are the two most durable IS-enabled competitive advantages
IT governance ensures IS investments align with business strategy — without governance, IS investments optimize locally and underperform strategically

learn.explanation: Platform businesses use IS to create two-sided markets connecting producers and consumers — Uber connects drivers and riders, Airbnb connects hosts and guests, App Store connects developers and users. The platform itself creates value through matching, trust mechanisms, and network effects. Build vs. buy decisions determine whether organizations develop custom IS or purchase commercial solutions — a strategic choice with long-term implications for flexibility and competitive differentiation.
learn.example: Apple's App Store is a platform IS that connects 34 million developers with 1.5 billion device users. Apple doesn't build most apps — it builds the infrastructure that makes app creation and distribution possible, takes a 15-30% revenue cut, and benefits from network effects as both sides grow. The IS that runs the App Store (developer tools, payment processing, review systems, search) is the competitive moat.
keyPoints: Platform IS creates value by reducing transaction costs between two groups who need to find and trust each other
Network effects make platforms naturally monopolistic — the leading platform typically captures most of the market
Build vs. buy: build when IS capability is a core differentiator; buy when IS is necessary but not strategic

learn.explanation: IS implementation is the process of deploying a new system into an organization — including technical installation, data migration, user training, and process redesign. The most common implementation failures are scope creep (requirements expand beyond original plan), inadequate testing, insufficient training, and resistance to process changes. Change management is the structured approach to transitioning people from current to future state.
learn.example: Hershey's 1999 SAP ERP implementation failed spectacularly — the company couldn't fulfill $100M in Halloween candy orders because the new system wasn't working and staff weren't trained. The technical implementation completed on schedule; the human and process implementation did not. Stock price dropped 8% in one day. The lesson is cited in every IS implementation textbook.
keyPoints: IS implementation fails most often at the people layer — technical completion does not equal successful deployment
Parallel running (operating old and new systems simultaneously) reduces risk but doubles workload during transition
Post-implementation review is consistently skipped and consistently valuable — capturing lessons learned improves future IS projects
